name: IronNet
summary: Network threat-detection company founded by former NSA director Gen. Keith Alexander, went public via SPAC in 2021 but revenue fell far short of projections; it furloughed staff, defaulted on an AWS bill, and filed for Chapter 7 liquidation in October 2023.
lesson: A prestigious founder and a hot SPAC market cannot substitute for durable revenue growth against projections made to public investors.

name: Cyren
summary: Email and web security/threat-intelligence vendor (formerly Commtouch) was delisted from Nasdaq and commenced insolvency proceedings in Israel in February 2023 after failing to find a buyer or new financing.
lesson: Legacy security vendors that fail to modernize their product and cost structure eventually lose to better-funded, cloud-native competitors.

name: Skybox Security
summary: Israeli attack-simulation and vulnerability-management vendor abruptly shut down in February 2024 and laid off its entire ~300-person staff after its shift to SaaS failed to gain traction; rival Tufin acquired select technology assets.
lesson: A large, late-stage funding round cannot rescue a company whose core product transition (on-prem to SaaS) doesn't land with customers.

name: NS8
summary: Fraud-prevention startup collapsed in September 2020 after founder/CEO Adam Rogas was charged by the SEC with fabricating customer revenue and bank statements to raise over $100M; he later pleaded guilty and was sentenced to five years in prison.
lesson: Fabricated metrics can inflate a funding round for a while, but fraud eventually collapses the company entirely once discovered.

name: Norse Corporation
summary: Threat-intelligence firm known for its live 'attack map' visualization imploded in early 2016 amid reports of a high cash-burn rate, an abrupt CEO ouster, and questions over the actual quality of its threat data.
lesson: Flashy demos and marketing cannot compensate for a threat-intelligence product that customers and analysts come to doubt is substantively accurate.

name: Cybereason
summary: EDR vendor once valued near $3B underwent repeated rounds of layoffs from 2022-2024, and by early 2025 its CEO sued major investors SoftBank and Liberty Strategic Capital alleging a board deadlock was pushing the company toward imminent bankruptcy.
lesson: Investor-board conflict over follow-on funding can bring a once-thriving company to the brink of collapse even when its product still has customers.

name: Damballa
summary: Network-based malware detection pioneer failed to meet revenue expectations and was sold to Core Security for under $10M in 2016, a fraction of the capital it had raised.
lesson: Being an early technology pioneer offers no protection if the company cannot convert that lead into sustainable revenue before better-funded rivals catch up.

name: Trustwave
summary: Managed security services and PCI-compliance vendor was bought by Singtel for $770M in 2015 but was sold off in 2024 to a Chertoff Group-affiliated fund for just $205M, wiping out roughly three-quarters of its value.
lesson: A large strategic acquisition price is no guarantee of durable value; managed security services businesses can still lose ground to more efficient competitors.

name: Good Technology
summary: Mobile device and data security vendor withdrew a planned IPO after its valuation cratered, and was ultimately sold to BlackBerry in 2015 for $425M — far below the $1B+ it had once expected.
lesson: Delaying an IPO while market sentiment and competitive position deteriorate can permanently destroy the exit value investors were counting on.

name: vArmour
summary: Micro-segmentation and cloud security startup that raised over $120M sold off its intellectual property assets in 2022 after failing to achieve product-market fit at scale.
lesson: Heavy venture funding for a technically sophisticated product cannot substitute for a clear, sellable use case that drives broad enterprise adoption.

name: Cyphort
summary: Advanced threat-detection vendor was acquired by Juniper Networks in 2017 for an undisclosed but reportedly modest sum after raising roughly $65M, well below investor expectations.
lesson: A crowded threat-detection market can force even well-funded, technically credible vendors into an underwhelming acqui-hire exit.

name: Threat Stack
summary: Cloud workload security vendor was acquired by F5 Networks in 2022 for a modest sum, and F5 discontinued the Threat Stack product entirely within about a year of the deal.
lesson: An acquisition by a larger platform vendor does not guarantee product survival if it doesn't fit the acquirer's long-term roadmap.

name: Bromium
summary: Endpoint isolation/micro-virtualization security vendor was acquired by HP Inc. in 2019 in a deal widely seen as underwhelming relative to the roughly $130M it had raised from investors.
lesson: Deep, hardware-adjacent security technology can be expensive to build and sell, and may not return venture-scale outcomes even with strong engineering.

name: CipherCloud
summary: Early cloud access security broker (CASB) pioneer was acquired by Lookout in 2022 for an undisclosed, reportedly low valuation after losing ground to competitors like Netskope and Skyhigh.
lesson: Being first to a security category is not enough; slower iteration than well-funded fast-followers can erase an early leader's advantage.

name: ZeroFox
summary: Digital risk protection/social media threat platform went public via SPAC in 2022 but its stock persistently traded far below the deal price, leading the company to be taken private by Haveli Investments in 2024-2025 at a steep discount.
lesson: SPAC mergers that lock in an ambitious public valuation can leave a fundamentally sound business unable to meet market expectations post-listing.

name: E8 Security
summary: Behavioral analytics/UEBA startup was acquired by VMware's Carbon Black in 2018 and its standalone product was subsequently discontinued and folded into the broader Carbon Black platform.
lesson: Niche analytics point products are often acquired mainly for talent and IP, with the original product line quietly retired.

name: Bay Dynamics
summary: Cyber risk analytics vendor that had raised tens of millions quietly wound down operations around 2019 after failing to scale its enterprise risk-analytics platform against larger competitors.
lesson: Enterprise risk-analytics products with long sales cycles can burn through funding before reaching the scale needed to survive independently.

name: FireEye Products (Trellix)
summary: FireEye's core network/endpoint security products business, once a market leader after its 2013 IPO, was sold off in 2021 for $1.2B to Symphony Technology Group and merged with McAfee Enterprise into Trellix, while the higher-margin Mandiant incident-response unit was separated and later sold to Google.
lesson: A pioneering security product can still lose its independent standing once growth slows and margins compress against nimbler cloud-native rivals.

name: Lacework
summary: Cloud security posture management (CNAPP) unicorn valued at $8.3B in 2021 was sold to Fortinet in 2024 for an estimated $200-230M, a roughly 97% collapse in valuation that wiped out venture returns.
lesson: A massive up-round valuation during a hype cycle can set a company up for a devastating down-round once growth expectations aren't met.

name: Cylance
summary: AI-driven endpoint protection pioneer was bought by BlackBerry for $1.4B in 2019, but BlackBerry sold the Cylance business to Arctic Wolf in 2024-2025 for just $160M in cash plus stock, a fraction of the original purchase price.
lesson: An acquirer overpaying for a hot AI-security narrative can end up divesting the asset years later for a fraction of the price, destroying value along the way.

name: CyberSponse
summary: Security orchestration and automated response (SOAR) vendor was hit by co-founder litigation and governance disputes before being acquired by FireEye in 2019 for a modest sum.
lesson: Internal founder disputes and governance breakdowns can force a distress sale even for a company in a growing product category.

name: Cloudmark
summary: Email and messaging security pioneer was acquired by Proofpoint in 2017 for a price well below its earlier private valuations, reflecting years of slowing growth in a maturing anti-spam market.
lesson: First-generation anti-spam/messaging security technology can become commoditized, eroding pricing power and exit value over time.

name: Barkly
summary: Endpoint protection startup was acquired by Malwarebytes in 2020 for an undisclosed, reportedly modest sum after struggling to differentiate in the crowded EDR/EPP market.
lesson: Endpoint security is a scale game; smaller vendors without a distinct wedge often end up as tuck-in acquisitions rather than standalone winners.

name: Fyde
summary: Zero-trust remote access startup was quietly acquired by Barracuda Networks in 2019 in a small, largely unpublicized deal after a short independent run.
lesson: Being early to the zero-trust access category doesn't guarantee independent survival if funding runs out before reaching scale.

name: Cygilant
summary: Managed detection and response / SOC-as-a-service provider (formerly EIQnetworks) wound down operations around 2020 after years of subscale growth in the managed security services market.
lesson: Managed security services businesses need real scale to cover 24/7 SOC costs; subscale providers can quietly run out of runway.

name: SecureWorks
funding: publicly traded (IPO 2016, majority owned by Dell)
summary: Managed detection and response provider IPO'd in 2016 at $14/share but its stock fell more than 90% over the following years as growth slowed, leading majority owner Dell/Sophos to take it private again at a steep discount to its IPO valuation.
lesson: A public listing doesn't lock in value; sustained underperformance can erase most of a company's IPO-era valuation over time.

name: Webroot
summary: Consumer/SMB antivirus and threat-intelligence vendor was bought by OpenText for $618M in 2019, but underperformed expectations so badly that OpenText later recorded a large impairment write-down on the business.
lesson: An acquirer's write-down years after a deal signals the original price assumed growth that the underlying security business could not deliver.

name: WhiteHat Security
summary: Application security testing pioneer was acquired by NTT Security in 2019 and later folded into Synopsys's software integrity business in 2022-2023 at a valuation reflecting years of slowing growth relative to newer appsec entrants.
lesson: Early leadership in application security testing erodes quickly once developer-first, automation-native competitors enter the market.

name: Digital Guardian
summary: Data loss prevention (DLP) vendor that raised over $200M and shelved earlier IPO ambitions was ultimately sold to HelpSystems (Fortra) in 2021 for terms reportedly well below its cumulative venture investment.
lesson: Data loss prevention is a capital-intensive, long-sales-cycle category where even well-funded vendors can struggle to generate venture-scale returns.

name: SecureAuth
summary: Adaptive authentication/identity vendor merged with Core Security in 2019 under private equity ownership, then underwent a distressed recapitalization in 2023 that reportedly wiped out most equity value for earlier investors.
lesson: Private-equity roll-ups in identity security can still fail if the combined business can't generate enough cash flow to service its structure.

name: Cofense
summary: Phishing-simulation and reporting vendor (formerly PhishMe) was bought by a private-equity consortium for $400M in 2018, then underwent repeated rounds of layoffs in subsequent years as growth slowed under PE ownership.
lesson: Private-equity ownership can squeeze cost structure hard when growth stalls, leading to repeated layoffs rather than reinvestment.

name: Cyveillance
summary: Early cyber threat-intelligence and brand-protection vendor lost momentum against newer entrants and was acquired by LookingGlass Cyber in 2015 for an undisclosed, reportedly modest sum.
lesson: First-mover threat-intelligence vendors can be leapfrogged by better-productized competitors and end up as tuck-in acquisitions.

name: LookingGlass Cyber Solutions
summary: Threat-intelligence platform vendor that raised over $100M was acquired by ZeroFox in 2023 for undisclosed terms after struggling to achieve the growth trajectory its funding had implied.
lesson: Large cumulative venture funding raises the bar for what counts as a successful exit; a modest acquisition after $100M+ raised is effectively a loss for investors.

name: Cavirin
summary: Cloud security posture management startup was quietly acquired by ColorTokens in 2021 for an undisclosed, reportedly modest sum in a crowded CSPM market dominated by larger platform vendors.
lesson: Entering a hot security category late, without a clear differentiator, often ends in a small tuck-in sale rather than independent growth.

name: CloudPassage
summary: Cloud workload security pioneer that raised over $90M was acquired by Fidelis Cybersecurity in 2021 for undisclosed terms, well after larger competitors had captured most of the cloud security posture management market.
lesson: Being an early cloud security category creator provides no lasting moat if larger, better-capitalized rivals out-execute on go-to-market.

name: RiskSense
summary: Vulnerability risk-management platform was acquired by Ivanti in 2021 for undisclosed, reportedly modest terms as part of Ivanti's broader roll-up strategy rather than as a standalone growth story.
lesson: Being absorbed into a private-equity-backed roll-up is often a sign a company could not sustain independent growth economics.

name: Cyberbit
summary: Israeli OT/ICS security and cyber-range training company underwent significant layoffs and strategic retrenchment in the early 2020s as the specialized OT security market proved smaller and slower-growing than projected.
lesson: Specialized security niches (like OT/ICS) can have a much smaller addressable market than the broader enterprise security narrative suggests to investors.

name: Pulse Secure
summary: Enterprise VPN vendor's flagship product became one of the most widely exploited attack vectors of 2019-2021 after critical vulnerabilities were used in major ransomware campaigns, badly damaging its reputation before it was folded into Ivanti in 2020.
lesson: Chronic, widely-exploited vulnerabilities in a security product can destroy customer trust faster than any competitive pressure.

name: CrowdStrike
summary: Cloud-native endpoint detection and response (EDR) leader went public in 2019 and grew into one of the largest pure-play cybersecurity companies by market cap, despite a major reputational setback from its July 2024 global outage incident.
lesson: A cloud-native architecture and strong platform network effects can build enduring enterprise market leadership even after a major operational crisis.

name: Palo Alto Networks
summary: Next-generation firewall pioneer went public in 2012 and grew via aggressive platform M&A into one of the largest cybersecurity companies in the world, spanning network, cloud, and SOC security.
lesson: Disciplined, platform-oriented acquisitions can compound a core product's success into a much broader enterprise security suite.

name: Fortinet
summary: Unified threat management/network security appliance vendor went public in 2009 and became a consistently profitable, large-scale cybersecurity company through vertically integrated hardware and software.
lesson: Owning custom silicon and vertically integrating hardware with software can produce durable margin advantages in network security.

name: Zscaler
summary: Cloud security service edge (SSE) pioneer went public in 2018 and became a category leader in cloud-delivered secure web gateway and zero-trust access as enterprises shifted away from perimeter firewalls.
lesson: Riding a genuine architectural shift (perimeter to cloud-delivered security) ahead of the market can create a durable first-mover platform.

name: SentinelOne
summary: AI-driven autonomous endpoint protection vendor went public in 2021 at one of the largest cybersecurity IPO valuations of that year and has continued to grow as a major CrowdStrike competitor.
lesson: A well-timed IPO during a strong cybersecurity market window can validate years of R&D investment in a differentiated detection engine.

name: Okta
summary: Cloud identity and access management pioneer went public in 2017 and became a category leader in workforce and customer identity, later acquiring Auth0 for $6.5B to expand into developer-first CIAM.
lesson: Being the neutral, cloud-native identity layer across thousands of SaaS apps creates strong switching costs and network effects.

name: CyberArk
summary: Privileged access management (PAM) pioneer went public in 2014 and became the dominant vendor in a security category that grew increasingly critical as breaches involving compromised credentials multiplied.
lesson: Owning a narrow but mission-critical security category (privileged credentials) can support durable, high-margin growth for decades.

name: Rapid7
summary: Vulnerability management and security analytics vendor went public in 2015 and grew into a broad detection-and-response platform through organic development and acquisitions like NetworkFax and DivvyCloud.
lesson: Expanding from a single strong product (vulnerability scanning) into an adjacent platform can sustain growth as the original market matures.

name: Qualys
summary: Cloud-based vulnerability management pioneer went public in 2012 and has remained a profitable, independent public company for over a decade in the vulnerability and compliance management space.
lesson: A capital-efficient path to profitability, rather than growth at all costs, can sustain decades of independent public-company life.

name: Tenable
summary: Vulnerability management vendor built on the Nessus scanner went public in 2018 and expanded into broader exposure management as enterprises consolidated vulnerability and cloud security tooling.
lesson: A widely trusted open-source-rooted scanning engine can be the foundation for a large, durable commercial security franchise.

name: Varonis
summary: Data security and insider-threat analytics vendor went public in 2014 and successfully transitioned its business model to a SaaS/subscription platform, sustaining growth as data security became a board-level priority.
lesson: A successful pivot from perpetual licensing to SaaS subscriptions can reinvigorate growth for an already-public security vendor.

name: Mimecast
summary: Email security and resilience vendor went public in 2015 and was later taken private by Permira in 2022 for approximately $5.8B, a strong outcome for a two-decade-old company in a mature category.
lesson: Email security remains a durable, must-have category, supporting steady growth and a strong eventual private-equity exit.

name: Proofpoint
summary: Email and human-risk security vendor went public in 2012 and was acquired by Thoma Bravo in 2021 for approximately $12.3B, one of the largest private-equity cybersecurity buyouts to date.
lesson: Consistent execution in a critical security category can culminate in a mega-buyout even after years as a mature public company.

name: Imperva
summary: Application and data security vendor went public in 2011, was taken private by Thoma Bravo in 2019 for $2.1B, and was later sold to Thales in 2023 for $3.6B, delivering strong returns across two ownership cycles.
lesson: A well-positioned security asset can generate value across multiple private-equity ownership cycles, not just a single exit.

name: Duo Security
summary: Two-factor authentication and zero-trust access startup was acquired by Cisco in 2018 for $2.35B, one of the highest capital-efficiency exits in cybersecurity given its relatively modest total funding.
lesson: A simple, well-designed product solving an urgent security problem (MFA) can produce an outsized exit relative to capital raised.

name: Carbon Black
summary: Endpoint detection and response vendor (formed via merger with Bit9) went public in 2018 and was acquired by VMware in 2019 for approximately $2.1B, becoming a core part of VMware's security portfolio.
lesson: Consolidating complementary endpoint security technologies through merger can create enough scale to attract a large strategic acquirer.

name: Darktrace
summary: AI-based threat-detection vendor went public on the London Stock Exchange in 2021 and was acquired by Thoma Bravo in 2024 for approximately $5.3B, despite earlier short-seller allegations about its sales metrics.
lesson: A strong private-equity exit can still be achieved even after a public company weathers public scrutiny over its growth accounting.

name: KnowBe4
summary: Security awareness training and phishing-simulation vendor went public in 2021 and was taken private by Vista Equity Partners in 2023 for approximately $4.6B, an exceptional outcome relative to minimal outside funding.
lesson: Bootstrapped or lightly-funded growth in a recurring-revenue security niche can still produce a multi-billion-dollar exit.

name: Sophos
summary: Endpoint and network security vendor was taken private by Thoma Bravo in 2020 for approximately $3.9B after decades as an independent public company on the London Stock Exchange.
lesson: Decades of steady execution in core endpoint security can culminate in a strong private-equity buyout even in a mature market.

name: McAfee
summary: Consumer and enterprise antivirus pioneer went through multiple ownership changes (Intel, IPO, Thoma Bravo) and its consumer business was taken private by a private-equity/family-office consortium in 2022 for approximately $14B.
lesson: A legacy security brand can be repeatedly restructured and resold across decades while still commanding a substantial valuation.

name: Check Point Software Technologies
summary: Israeli firewall and network security pioneer has remained an independent, consistently profitable public company on Nasdaq for nearly three decades, expanding into cloud and endpoint security.
lesson: Disciplined profitability and steady product expansion can sustain independence for decades in a market full of acquisition targets.

name: Trend Micro
summary: Antivirus and cloud security vendor has remained a thriving, independent, publicly traded company for decades, successfully expanding from consumer antivirus into enterprise cloud and hybrid-cloud security.
lesson: Successfully repositioning a legacy consumer security brand toward enterprise cloud security can sustain long-term independent growth.

name: Splunk
summary: Machine data analytics and SIEM/security platform went public in 2012 and was acquired by Cisco in 2024 for approximately $28B, one of the largest cybersecurity/data-platform acquisitions ever.
lesson: Becoming the default data platform underlying enterprise security operations can produce a massive strategic acquisition even after decades as a public company.

name: Ping Identity
summary: Enterprise identity and access management vendor went public in 2019 and was acquired by Thoma Bravo in 2022 for approximately $2.8B, consolidating with ForgeRock under private-equity ownership.
lesson: Identity management's criticality to enterprise IT makes it a reliable target for private-equity consolidation at attractive valuations.

name: ForgeRock
summary: Open-standards-based identity platform went public in 2021 and was acquired by Thoma Bravo/Ping Identity in 2023 for approximately $2.3B, giving investors a solid return within two years of its IPO.
lesson: An open, developer-friendly approach to enterprise identity can command a strong acquisition premium even in a highly consolidated space.

name: Recorded Future
summary: Threat-intelligence platform vendor grew into an industry leader over 15 years and was acquired by Mastercard in 2024 for approximately $2.65B, a strong outcome for a relatively capital-efficient security company.
lesson: Deep, defensible threat-intelligence data assets can attract strategic buyers well outside the traditional cybersecurity industry.

name: RiskIQ
summary: External attack surface management and threat-intelligence vendor was acquired by Microsoft in 2021 for a reported $500M+, giving Microsoft's security suite a strong external-visibility capability.
lesson: A relatively lean funding history combined with a genuinely differentiated dataset can lead to a strong acquisition by a hyperscaler.

name: Area 1 Security
summary: Preemptive email/phishing security vendor was acquired by Cloudflare in 2022 for approximately $162M, expanding Cloudflare's security portfolio into email protection.
lesson: A focused, differentiated security niche can be a natural bolt-on acquisition for a larger platform expanding its security surface area.

name: Signal Sciences
summary: Web application firewall (WAF) and RASP vendor was acquired by Fastly in 2020 for approximately $775M, giving Fastly a strong application security offering alongside its CDN business.
lesson: A modern, developer-friendly rearchitecting of a mature category (WAF) can command a premium acquisition price from an adjacent infrastructure player.

name: Sourcefire
summary: Intrusion detection/prevention pioneer built on the open-source Snort engine went public in 2007 and was acquired by Cisco in 2013 for approximately $2.7B, a landmark cybersecurity exit at the time.
lesson: An open-source community engine at the core of a commercial product can build enormous trust and adoption, driving a premium strategic acquisition.

name: Wiz
summary: Cloud security posture management (CNAPP) startup grew to roughly $1B in ARR within about five years, rejected a $23B Google acquisition offer in 2024, then agreed to be acquired by Google for approximately $32B in 2025-2026, the largest pure cybersecurity acquisition on record.
lesson: Extremely rapid, capital-efficient growth in a hot category can give founders the leverage to walk away from a huge offer and secure an even larger one later.

name: Netskope
summary: Cloud security service edge (SSE) vendor went public in September 2025, pricing its IPO at a roughly $7.3 billion valuation and raising over $900M, one of the largest cybersecurity IPOs of 2025.
lesson: Sustained execution through a long private-funding runway can still culminate in a strong public-market debut once IPO market conditions turn favorable.
